instruction: How can I handle nighttime feedings so the baby resettles easily afterward?
output: Keep the room dark, warm, and quiet for nighttime feedings and avoid using a bright light. If the diaper is soiled or wet, change it quickly and without fanfare before feeding and put the baby right back to sleep afterward.

instruction: Can eating more food make me produce more milk?
output: Eating more won’t make more milk, and not eating enough won’t make less milk. Calories are pretty simple in that way.

instruction: Should I rinse my baby’s clothes extra to avoid skin irritation?
output: Yes. Double-rinse all baby clothes, bedding, blankets, and other washable items before exposing the child to them. For the first few months, do your infant’s wash separately.

instruction: How can I lengthen my night when I have a new baby who wakes me up?
output: Go to bed earlier and stay in bed later when possible, avoid clock-watching, get help with household tasks, and try to rest when the baby sleeps; these strategies can lengthen your effective nightly sleep.

instruction: Can I give acetaminophen to my very young baby for fever or irritability?
output: Acetaminophen will lower an elevated temperature and calm her if she’s irritable, but you should give it to a baby this young only on your pediatrician’s advice. Do not use aspirin.

instruction: How should clinicians decide whether to reduce their fee for postpartum therapy?
output: Clinicians should weigh whether reducing the fee makes the service more obtainable or diminishes its value. This decision needs to be made case by case, teasing out clients who genuinely need financial adjustments from those who would simply prefer a lower fee.

instruction: What is early morning wakening and what does it indicate?
output: Early morning wakening is waking earlier than desired or having middle-of-the-night disruptions; it is commonly considered a hallmark symptom of depression.

instruction: Are musical toys okay for my 5-month-old, and any precautions?
output: Musical toys such as bells, maracas, and tambourines are listed as appropriate for a four-to-seven-month-old. Make sure none of the parts can become loose.

instruction: My supply dropped around five months of exclusive pumping—what might cause that?
output: Supply dips around four to six months are common for exclusive pumpers. One cause can be overfeeding by bottle; check the baby’s weight gain to assess that. If overfeeding is not the issue, you may need to take steps to boost production.

instruction: What are the first signs that my milk is starting to come back?
output: Early signs include breast tenderness and fullness. You may notice drops when expressing that are initially clear and later become white, with gradual increases in volume over time.

instruction: How much milk should I put in each container before freezing?
output: Fill each container with about 2 ounces (60 ml) to minimize waste. Because milk expands when frozen, leave some space at the top if you plan to freeze.

instruction: What are simple ways to fit small pleasures or hobbies into a busy day with a nursing baby?
output: Try reading in five-minute spurts or listening to an audiobook or podcast while you nurse or wash dishes; record TV shows and watch short segments later; or have a partner take the baby for a short outing so you can enjoy focused time.

instruction: How does a therapist gain the judgment to know what to do in postpartum therapy?
output: Clinical judgment and wisdom develop from years of practice, supervised experience, taking appropriate risks, learning from mistakes, and partnering with clients to integrate their lived experience with clinical knowledge.

instruction: Which foods should I never give my baby because of choking risk?
output: Never offer foods your baby might choke on. Examples given are whole nuts, uncut grapes or cherry tomatoes, or raw carrots or celery. Also avoid any food that truly requires chewing, even if the baby has teeth.

instruction: My toddler keeps hitting and biting other kids. How should I respond?
output: Make sure he understands that hitting, biting, and kicking are not acceptable. Show displeasure with your facial expression and tone, then remove him from the situation. Use firm but gentle guidance and, when appropriate, briefly isolate him with no attention or toys (time-out). Consistency and calm enforcement of limits help.

instruction: If my therapist cries when I tell her about my loss, is that an appropriate response to disclose her sadness?
output: Yes. A therapist might appropriately reveal sadness in the moment if a client’s account of a loss moves her to tears; by contrast, she should not disclose unresolved personal grief if she is still recovering and that disclosure would interfere with the client’s care.

instruction: Can I negotiate nursing times with a toddler when we're out or busy?
output: Yes. With a toddler, nursing can often be negotiated—for example, asking them to wait a minute, to wait until you get home, or offering to nurse while you read. Negotiation and compromise help the child learn to separate needs from wants while still responding when the child truly can’t wait (for example if they are hurt).

instruction: If I can’t get my pumped milk to my baby because I’m deployed or traveling, does pumping still help?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. In some situations you may be able to keep up your supply by pumping even if you aren’t able to get the milk to your baby; temporary or partial weaning may be necessary in other situations.

instruction: Are SSRIs the first choice for treating postpartum depression?
output: Yes. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) are commonly used as first-line medication treatment for postpartum depression.

instruction: Why do mother and baby often get sleepy after the first nursing session?
output: Oxytocin released during the first nursing session promotes drowsiness in both mother and baby, and many pairs rest or semi-doze for an hour or two while feeding continues intermittently.

instruction: When is the best time in pregnancy to get the Tdap vaccine to protect my baby from pertussis?
output: The ideal time is between the twenty-seventh and thirty-sixth weeks of pregnancy. It is recommended for every pregnant woman during each pregnancy. If the vaccine is not given during pregnancy, it should be given immediately after delivery.

instruction: My surgeon says I’ll need general anesthesia for a procedure. Do I have to wean my baby?
output: No. You generally do not need to stop breastfeeding or discard milk for a standard general anesthetic. Once you are awake, anesthesia is essentially cleared from your blood and milk. These recommendations apply to full-term and healthy infants; premature or ill infants may need individual consideration.

instruction: When I breastfeed, I sometimes feel strong cramps and a gush of blood — is that normal?
output: Yes. Breastfeeding releases oxytocin which causes uterine contractions; you may notice cramps and occasional gushes of blood during feeds. The cramps usually fade over time, though they can be strong at first.

instruction: Will my prescribing doctor tell me about possible SSRI side effects?
output: Ideally the prescribing physician will inform patients about potential side effects before starting an SSRI and discuss what to expect and how to manage them.

instruction: My newborn's eyes are blue — will they stay that color?
output: Many Caucasian newborns have blue eyes but this may change over the first year. If a baby’s eyes are going to turn brown, they’ll probably become muddy-looking during the first six months; however, if they’re still blue at that time, they’ll probably remain so. Infants with darker heritage generally have brown eyes at birth and tend to retain that color.

instruction: Is it okay to play with my baby right after a feeding?
output: Do not jostle or play vigorously with the baby immediately after feeding.

instruction: How often do solely breastfed babies usually feed in a 24-hour period?
output: Solely breastfed babies usually feed eight to twelve times in each twenty-four-hour period.

instruction: How can I tell if my pump flange is the right size for me?
output: Use comfort and milk flow as guides: the flange should feel comfortable and allow good milk flow. You may need to try several sizes or consult a lactation specialist or store with fittings. Nipples and breast tissue can change over time, so sizes that didn’t fit before may work later.

instruction: What should I ask women who have given birth in more than one setting?
output: Ask what differences they experienced in the birth process, their satisfaction, breastfeeding success, and their relationship with the children, to get practical perspectives on different settings.

instruction: At 15 months, what are average weight and height for girls and boys?
output: At fifteen months, the average girl weighs about 23 pounds (10.5 kg) and is almost 30.5 inches (77 cm) tall; the average boy weighs about 24.5 pounds (11 kg) and is about 31 inches (78 cm) tall.

instruction: Which signs in the second to fourth weeks after birth mean I should notify the pediatrician?
output: Notify your pediatrician if during weeks two to four your baby: sucks poorly and feeds slowly; doesn’t blink when shown a bright light; doesn’t focus and follow a nearby object moving side to side; rarely moves arms and legs or seems stiff; seems excessively loose in the limbs or floppy; has a lower jaw that trembles constantly even when not crying or excited; or doesn’t respond to loud sounds.

instruction: How should I hold my breast to help the baby latch properly?
output: Hold the breast with your thumb above the areola and your fingers and palm underneath it. Some gentle compression can help form a surface for latch-on. Keep the nipple level or pointed slightly up and keep your fingers clear of the areola so the baby can grasp it; your fingers should be no closer than 2 inches from the base of the nipple.

instruction: What should I ask visitors to do before holding my newborn?
output: Ask visitors to call in advance, wash their hands before holding the baby, and keep their visits brief until you’re back to a regular schedule. If the baby seems unsettled by the attention, don’t let anyone not close to you hold him.

instruction: My baby pulls on and off the breast and seems to salivate a lot. Are these signs I should consider reflux evaluation?
output: Yes. Pulling on and off as if feeding is painful and excess salivation or near‑constant congestion/rumbly breathing are among reflux signs. If symptoms persist or the baby seems bothered, ask your pediatrician about evaluation and possible referral.

instruction: What can I learn from the times my baby fusses and I soothe her?
output: These spells help both of you learn about each other: you’ll learn how she likes to be rocked, which faces or voices she enjoys, and what she likes to look at. She’ll learn what to do to get your response, how hard you’ll try to please her, and where your limits lie.

instruction: How should I prepare hot dogs, grapes, or chunks of cheese for my baby?
output: Hot dogs, grapes, chunks of cheese, or meat sticks should be cut lengthwise and then into smaller pieces before being fed to a child of this age.

instruction: I have a cold — should I still breastfeed my baby?
output: Continue to breastfeed when you have a cold, since doing so supplies the baby with nourishment and protective antibodies. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: My friend put an amber teething necklace on her infant. Do amber necklaces help with teething pain?
output: Amber teething necklaces do not relieve pain and have caused cases of choking and strangulation. Never leave anything around your baby’s neck.

instruction: Should family and others around my newborn be vaccinated against pertussis?
output: Yes. It is recommended that everyone around your baby—grandparents, parents, and children—make sure they are current on pertussis vaccines. Anyone in close contact with your baby should be vaccinated regardless of age, and make sure other children in the family are up to date on their Tdap immunizations.

instruction: If I’m too tired to think straight and can’t report how much I sleep, is that a sign of depression?
output: A new mother with depression may respond with somber haziness (for example, 'I don’t know' or 'I’m too tired to think straight'), which differs from the typical lighthearted complaints about lack of sleep and can be a clue to clinical depression.

instruction: Will holding my baby off the breast prevent bad habits or spoilage?
output: Deliberately withholding feeding can interfere with infant physiology, is not shown to provide benefits, and can slow weight gain over time. Putting your baby to the breast when they cry will not spoil them.

instruction: If my child needs to see a pediatric subspecialist, will my pediatrician need to approve it?
output: Managed care plans often require the primary care pediatrician to act as a gatekeeper and give approval before your child can see a pediatric subspecialist. Without that approval, you may have to pay for part or all of those services out of pocket. Read your plan materials to determine how referrals are handled.

instruction: How long are the breaks I should ask for to express milk during the workday?
output: You’ll need a couple of twenty-minute breaks each day in addition to your lunch break; that’s about the same amount of time most smokers take.

instruction: Can I extend my maternity leave if my employer won't give more paid time?
output: You may be able to arrange an extension, though it is often unpaid; planning ahead, discussing options with your employer, and considering unpaid leave or flexible scheduling can sometimes extend time at home.

instruction: Should I comfort my baby right away after I scold her?
output: Don’t be too quick to comfort after scolding. Wait a minute or two before reassuring her; otherwise she won’t know whether she really did something wrong. Also react immediately when you see her heading into trouble rather than waiting several minutes.

instruction: I want to exercise while breastfeeding. Any simple things I should do so nursing and workouts work well together?
output: Breastfeed before you work out so your breasts are less full. Wear a supportive bra (some women wear two) and use breast pads if leaking is an issue. If your baby dislikes nursing when you’re sweaty, take a quick shower or towel-dry before you nurse.

instruction: How does a baby learn cause and effect around four to five months?
output: Between about four and five months he begins to notice cause and effect—for example, kicking a mattress and seeing the crib shake or hitting a rattle and hearing a sound. Once he understands he can cause reactions, he experiments more, such as dropping things to see what happens.

instruction: What should I do if the baby gets tense or starts gulping during a bottle?
output: If he gets tense or gulps, tilt baby and bottle slightly forward so the milk drains away. Allow and encourage pauses so he can learn to pace his sucking and breathing.

instruction: Is it okay to make bottle feedings just functional when my baby needs supplements at home?
output: Yes. If your baby is supplemented at home, make the bottle-feedings strictly business and make nursings the time of cuddles and cozying to help maintain the breastfeeding relationship.

instruction: Her belly feels hard and swollen and she hasn’t had a bowel movement for two days. What does that mean?
output: If the abdomen feels swollen and hard and the baby has not had a bowel movement for more than one or two days or is vomiting, call your pediatrician. Most likely it is due to gas or constipation, but it could signal a more serious intestinal problem.

instruction: My baby coughs and sputters when feeding. Is this serious?
output: If the baby drinks very fast, she may cough and sputter; this type of coughing should stop as the feeding routine becomes familiar. It may also relate to how strongly or fast a breastfeeding mother’s milk comes down. If she coughs persistently or routinely gags during feedings, consult the pediatrician because these symptoms could indicate an underlying problem in the lungs or digestive tract.

instruction: If I used opioids while pregnant, what could happen to my newborn?
output: Opioids like OxyContin and heroin can cause withdrawal symptoms in newborns that may keep them hospitalized for weeks or more.

instruction: Are questions about newborn worries silly to ask leaders or other parents?
output: No question is silly. Many mothers contact breastfeeding leaders or experienced parents about common issues in the first few days; getting answers helps you become confident and fluent in caring for your baby.

instruction: If I’m already nursing another baby, can I use hormonal treatments to build a supply for an adoptive baby?
output: Hormonal protocols often suppress lactation initially and therefore are generally not suitable for someone who is already nursing another child. Discuss options and safety with your healthcare provider because certain hormones are not safe for everyone.

instruction: What should I do if I think I have an abscess?
output: An abscess requires prompt medical care. It is usually drained by a medical professional with a needle or a small incision and you will probably be put on antibiotics. You may have a small drain placed in the incision to keep the inside from sealing too soon.

instruction: Does the baby need to touch me in a specific way?
output: Let your baby’s whole front touch your whole front so their body is molded to yours.

instruction: When do babies start sharing family foods and how much should they eat?
output: By nine months most babies are eating some solid foods, and over time they tend to share most of the foods the family eats. The amounts may still be tiny at first, and other foods gradually become a larger part of their nutrition.

instruction: How much will my baby’s length and head grow between four and seven months?
output: Between four and seven months, a baby’s length typically increases by about 2 inches (5 cm) and head circumference by about 1 inch (2.5 cm).

instruction: What finger foods are safe to offer my baby?
output: Finger foods include small pieces of steamed vegetables, soft fruit such as bananas, well-cooked whole-grain pasta, small pieces of whole-wheat bread, chicken, scrambled eggs, or whole-grain cereals. Always watch her for choking while she eats.

instruction: People offer to help but I don't want to be a burden — what should I do when help is offered?
output: Accept offers of help when you can. Say yes to practical tasks people offer and try to connect with someone who will listen; joining support groups can also help.

instruction: If a baby has high bilirubin on the first day, should I assume it's normal newborn jaundice?
output: No. Babies usually begin to build up bilirubin from red cell breakdown at two to three days of age; high bilirubin on the first day suggests another cause and should be evaluated.

instruction: What kinds of sleep problems do postpartum women commonly describe?
output: Postpartum women commonly describe early morning wakening (waking early or middle-of-night disruptions), initial insomnia (difficulty falling asleep), and fragmented sleep measured by WASO.

instruction: My newborn cries a lot — why do babies cry?
output: Crying is a normal communication method for infants. It signals needs such as hunger, discomfort, or the need for closeness, helps regulate stimulation, and allows the baby to release tension.

instruction: Who does a postpartum woman need to help her through this time?
output: A postpartum woman needs a refuge where she can find support, guidance, reassurance, and clarity—a place that helps her feel like herself again.

instruction: What might help me decide to get support for postpartum depression?
output: Reframing the decision to seek support as a sign of strength rather than weakness can help some women refocus and proceed with greater conviction.

instruction: What should I do if a relative loses interest after telling her own parenting story?
output: Invite them to share their story without interruption; sometimes once they’ve had a chance to tell it they lose interest and tension eases.

instruction: What childbirth education programs could I look into?
output: Programs to consider include Lamaze, the Bradley Method, the International Childbirth Education Association (ICEA), and Hypnobirthing. Independent childbirth classes may also offer a broad range of options.

instruction: I've tried changing settings and flange and it still hurts. Who can I contact for help?
output: If none of these ideas helps, contact a lactation consultant, a La Leche League Leader, another experienced pumping parent, the place where you bought the pump, or the pump company’s hotline for troubleshooting and support.

instruction: What is the first of the 'Three Keeps' when breastfeeding isn't working?
output: The first 'Keep' is to keep your milk flowing. Early on your breasts are planning for future milk production and the amount of milk you remove tells them how much you’ll need. If the baby isn’t nursing, start hand-expressing or pumping, use massage and breast compressions, and express frequently so your breasts are ready when the baby can nurse.

instruction: What can I do if my baby fusses and won’t latch in the baby-led position?
output: If he fusses, try calming him by holding him vertically for a minute or two and maybe walking and talking to him, then try again. You can also bring his lower face into contact with your breast to help him focus and support his stability while he searches.

instruction: My toddler and baby fight over toys. Do young children naturally share?
output: Children under three aren’t capable of sharing without adult prodding and, in most cases, direct intervention. An adult should always be watching when children play together; encourage tandem play with each child having their own toys.

instruction: How can I reduce my infant's chance of getting a serious RSV infection?
output: Have people wash their hands with warm water and soap before picking up and holding your baby. Reduce close contact with people who have runny noses or other sicknesses. Limit siblings’ contact with the infant when they have a cold and make sure they wash their hands frequently. Keep your baby away from crowded areas where she’ll have close contact with people who may be sick. Don’t smoke around your baby or allow smoking near her. Continue to breastfeed when you have a cold, since it supplies nourishment and protective antibodies.

instruction: If my induction isn’t getting labor going but my membranes are still intact, what can I do?
output: If labor doesn’t start and your membranes are intact, you may have the option to go home and try again later, depending on your clinical situation and your provider’s plan. Discuss safety considerations and follow-up instructions with your care team.

instruction: My baby needed a respirator at first and his weight dropped. What helped the mother in the story prepare to breastfeed later?
output: In that example, the mother pumped frequently (every three hours or more) so she would have milk when the baby was ready. Pumping helped her feel she was contributing; later she began skin-to-skin care and moved toward breastfeeding as the baby’s condition improved.

instruction: Are there recommended books for latching approaches I can read?
output: Recommended books include Breastfeeding Made Simple by Nancy Mohrbacher and Kathleen Kendall-Tackett; The Latch by Jack Newman and Teresa Pitman; Mother-Baby Experiences of Nurturing by Suzanne Colson; and Supporting Sucking Skills in Breastfeeding Infants edited by Catherine Watson Genna.

instruction: Is it okay to not pump at work and use formula while breastfeeding at home?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Using formula when you’re at work and breastfeeding at home is certainly better than weaning completely to formula, but it’s the least desirable choice because the baby is exposed to the risks of formula-feeding and the mother is likely to have problems keeping her milk supply going; maximizing breastfeeding when you are together will help.

instruction: Is it possible to resume sex just a few days or weeks after birth?
output: Experiences vary: some people reported resuming intercourse as early as three days or four weeks, while others waited months; timing differs from person to person.

instruction: Is baby talk bad and should I stop using it completely?
output: It is easy to fall into the habit of baby talk, but you should try mixing your conversations with adult language and eventually phase out baby talk.

instruction: Will my baby bite me while nursing? I'm worried about being hurt.
output: Many babies never bite, some bite once or twice, and a few go through a biting phase. When a baby clamps, teeth or no teeth, they usually put their tongue out of the way first so they can’t bite themselves. A baby also cannot suck and bite at the same time, so while actively nursing you are generally safe.

instruction: Is it normal for nipples to be sensitive in the first few days?
output: Yes. Nipple tenderness or sensitivity is common in the early postpartum days as breastfeeding is established, but severe or ongoing pain is not normal and should be addressed.

instruction: When do babies usually drop their morning nap, and what changes should I make when that happens?
output: Around 10 to 12 months the morning nap begins to taper for some babies, and by about 12 months some may drop the morning nap. When that happens, you can move nighttime bedtime slightly earlier (about 20 to 30 minutes) and start the afternoon nap a little sooner.

instruction: I'm afraid I'll never feel better after childbirth. Is that a common fear that drives people to therapy?
output: Fear of never feeling better and being terrified to know they will get better are common reasons women seek specialized therapy after childbirth.

instruction: Are breastfed babies less likely to get sick than formula‑fed babies if I take them out?
output: Breastfeeding provides immune benefits that can reduce some illness risks. A breastfed baby is generally well protected and can go out when you feel ready, while minimizing contact with sick people.

instruction: Are premature babies more likely to have problems with bilirubin?
output: Yes. Premature or ill babies are at higher risk of accumulating too much bilirubin than healthy, full-term babies.

instruction: When should I offer to nurse to prevent fussiness?
output: One idea is to offer to nurse about an hour before he usually gets upset. Also, doing Carry, Contact, Walk, and Talk before crying starts can reduce crying overall.

instruction: Is it okay to keep using cute names like 'piggies' instead of the real words?
output: You should teach the correct names for objects and body parts and stop using cute substitutes such as 'piggies' when you really mean toes.

instruction: Are there situations where a chronic illness does not mean I have to wean?
output: Yes. Many chronic illnesses are improved by or unaffected by breastfeeding. For example, people with diabetes often find breastfeeding lowers their insulin needs. Discuss your specific condition with your clinician to determine compatibility with nursing.

instruction: What are gentle ways to help my baby stop using a nipple shield?
output: Remove the shield after the baby is fed, dozing, and contentedly sucking so he may search for the nipple. Try removing the shield in the middle of a nursing session at a calm time; if he gets upset, give him the shield and try again later. With an older baby you may need to wait a day or two between tries. Rinse your nipple and areola and coat them with your milk if the baby seems bothered by the change in taste. Do not cut away the tip of the shield.

instruction: Should I start antibiotics right away if I think I have mastitis?
output: Talk to your healthcare provider. Depending on your symptoms and exam, they may recommend a trial of conservative measures first or start antibiotics; seek medical advice rather than treating empirically on your own.

instruction: If I try to get my baby to wait longer between feeds so he sleeps more, could that cause problems?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Some babies naturally cut back feedings, but encouraging a baby to cut back can lead to early weaning.

instruction: Are La Leche League meetings useful when nursing a toddler?
output: Many mothers find La Leche League meetings or playgroups with other nursing mothers helpful at this stage. Some groups offer toddler-focused meetings and support on topics like social etiquette and common nursing concerns.

instruction: Is it okay to put my baby in a sling and go for a short walk soon after birth?
output: Yes. When the weather is suitable, wearing your baby in a sling for short walks can feel good; fresh air and gentle movement often help both parent and baby.

instruction: My baby seems to slump down and flop in the sling — is that okay?
output: No. The carrier should be snug so the baby is held upright and supported rather than flopping loosely. Slumping can allow the head to fall forward, which risks airway obstruction, so adjust the sling to bring the baby higher and more upright.

instruction: What should I do if my child still isn't walking at 18 months?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. A child who does not walk by age eighteen months should be evaluated by his pediatrician.

instruction: My baby makes a consistent sound for milk. Should I treat that as a real word?
output: Yes. If she uses a consistent sound reliably to request milk, respond to it and model the correct word back to her (for example, say "milk") to help her learn the association.

instruction: Is it okay to give a grieving mother a hug during a session?
output: A hug can be appropriate in some situations but can also be misinterpreted or violate personal space. Clinicians should be mindful, consider boundaries, and choose timing carefully; a hug may be more appropriate at a moment that provides closure, such as at the end of a session.

instruction: My older child keeps wanting to nurse the new baby. Will that last?
output: An older sibling who wants to try nursing again will quickly lose interest.

instruction: How should I think about choosing a health care provider for breastfeeding support?
output: Look for a provider whose style fits you and who offers support consistent with your breastfeeding goals. If conversations with a provider don’t feel right, it’s reasonable to seek another clinician.

instruction: If my baby is likely to have feeding difficulties, should I express colostrum during pregnancy?
output: Yes. If you anticipate feeding difficulties, expressing and storing colostrum during pregnancy can provide a ready supply for the baby after birth.

instruction: I use marijuana to help with nausea — is that okay during pregnancy?
output: Research is limited, but what we do know suggests marijuana use by mothers may be dangerous to the baby’s developing brain. Women who are pregnant, planning to become pregnant, or nursing should avoid marijuana use. If you are using marijuana to treat nausea, talk to your doctor about safer alternatives.

instruction: What snack options does the sample menu suggest?
output: Snack suggestions include a slice of toast or a whole-wheat muffin with 1–2 tablespoons of cream cheese or peanut butter, 1/2 cup yogurt with cut fruit, 1–2 ounces of cubed or string cheese, or 2–3 tablespoons of fruit or berries, served with water or 1/2 cup of whole or 2% milk.

instruction: Is it normal for toddlers to nurse their dolls or stuffed animals?
output: Yes. Breastfed children often imitate nursing with dolls and stuffed animals. They learn the behavior early by watching and it’s a common part of play.

instruction: Should my toddler eat only while seated and supervised?
output: Yes. Ensure meals and snacks are eaten while seated and supervised to reduce choking risk. Teach the child to finish a mouthful before speaking.

instruction: Can I get a tattoo while I'm still breastfeeding?
output: Tattooing during breastfeeding carries an infection risk; keeping the wound clean reduces that risk. Many professionals suggest waiting until after the child’s first birthday or until after weaning to allow your body time to recover from childbirth; many tattooists decline to tattoo pregnant or breastfeeding clients.

instruction: Does co-sleeping change how often babies breastfeed at night and how much mothers sleep?
output: Research indicates co-sleeping babies tend to breastfeed more frequently and for longer nighttime periods than babies who sleep alone, and some studies report breastfeeding mothers who bedshare obtain more total sleep than mothers whose babies sleep in another room.

instruction: My newborn cried a lot right after birth and didn’t try to nurse. Is that okay?
output: Yes. Some babies cry first as they transition from the womb; soothing, stroking, and skin-to-skin contact often help them settle and begin sucking within the first hour.

instruction: What is the “ooze method” for putting a sleeping baby down?
output: The ooze method is to nurse until the child is asleep, remain lying there a few more minutes, then very slowly slide away—toe by toe—hoping the child does not wake as you leave.

instruction: How much milk is included at each meal in the sample menu?
output: The example menu provides about 1/2 cup of whole or 2% milk at breakfast, 1/2 cup at lunch, 1/2 cup at dinner, and water or 1/2 cup of milk with snacks.

instruction: I feel guilty about disciplining my baby. Is spanking ever OK?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Physical punishment is always inappropriate: never spank or strike your child. Spanking teaches aggression, may stop behavior short term, can lead to physical injury, undermines communication, and weakens her sense of security. The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends time-outs when the child is older instead of spankings—putting the misbehaving child in a quiet place for a few minutes away from other people, TV, electronics, or books, then explaining why the behavior was unacceptable.

instruction: Do I need to pump as often as a nursing baby if I'm exclusively pumping?
output: Many exclusively pumping parents follow a schedule similar to frequent nursing: newborns nurse about 8–12 times per day, and many pumpers aim for roughly 10 sessions daily early on and not fewer than eight. After about six weeks some parents can reduce sessions if supply remains stable.

instruction: If I have a very specific question about my baby’s care, who should I ask?
output: Questions specific to your baby and her alone are best answered by your own pediatrician.

instruction: Do the titles 'lactation consultant' or 'breastfeeding specialist' guarantee expertise?
output: No. Those titles are not standardized and do not guarantee a specific level of training; ask about a person's credentials and experience. Look for an IBCLC when you need certified expertise.

instruction: Would making some toys off-limits help his possessiveness?
output: Yes. Selecting particularly prized items and making them off-limits to others may help toddlers feel they have some control and make them less possessive about other belongings.

instruction: Can I give my baby avocado, banana, or small pieces of apple?
output: Yes. Suitable starter fruits include avocado, banana, and small pieces of apple (peeled, grated, or lightly cooked). Offer them in textures and sizes the baby can safely handle.

instruction: Can I start sleep training so my baby sleeps through the night now?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Right now most babies still get a hefty portion of their calories at night. Unless a baby has chosen to sleep through the night on their own, trying to make them sleep through the night can slow the baby's rate of growth and risks early weaning.

instruction: My baby was born not breathing and had meconium — what is meconium aspiration and can it happen in babies born past their due date?
output: Meconium aspiration occurs when a newborn inhales meconium (fetal stool) into the airway. It is a recognized risk in postmature (post-term) infants.

instruction: Can my four-month-old get a flu shot now, or do we have to wait?
output: Once your child reaches six months, he will be old enough for a seasonal flu shot. Until then, other family members—parents and older siblings—should receive theirs to help protect the baby. There are antiviral medications for influenza, but not all are appropriate for newborns and very young children, and they may not be as effective as prevention via immunization.

instruction: If we have a waterbed, is a family bed less safe?
output: Having a waterbed is listed as an example of a risk factor that can make a family bed less safe.

instruction: Does sleeping with my baby affect how long I might breastfeed?
output: Research has found that babies assigned to sleep apart in the first days were about half as likely to be breastfeeding at four months as those sleeping with their mothers; babies in sidecars had intermediate rates. Early frequency of milk removal can influence breastfeeding duration.

instruction: I can't breastfeed — can bottle-feeding give the same closeness?
output: It makes sense to feel uncertain about this. Formula provides appropriate nutrition and both approaches are safe and healthy for your baby. You can achieve similar feelings of closeness during bottle-feedings by rocking, cuddling, stroking, and gazing into your baby’s eyes.

instruction: Should I store my newborn's cord blood in a private bank "just in case"?
output: The AAP discourages storing cord blood at private banks for later personal or family use as a general insurance policy. They encourage families to consider donating cord blood to banks for other individuals in need. There are no accurate statistics on the likelihood a child will need his or her own stored cells.

instruction: How did the recommendation to place babies on their backs change SIDS deaths?
output: Since the American Academy of Pediatrics recommended placing babies on their backs in 1992, deaths from SIDS have declined. Before the recommendation more than 5,000 babies died from SIDS each year in the United States; now deaths have declined to about 2,300 per year.

instruction: If I breastfeed within half an hour of birth, will that help me physically?
output: Breastfeeding within thirty minutes after birth may reduce postpartum hemorrhage.

instruction: My doctor only gave me an anti-anxiety medicine for agitation — is that enough?
output: Treating agitation only with an anxiolytic may provide short-term relief, but these medications do not typically address the core symptoms of depression; a comprehensive plan including an antidepressant and therapy is often needed.

instruction: How should a clinician handle it when their attention wanders during a session?
output: When attention wanders, clinicians should regroup, refocus on the client, and later explore the diversion in supervision or personal therapy so the client continues to receive full attention.

instruction: How far should a clinician 'step inside' a client's pain during grief work?
output: Clinicians should empathize and stay with the client's pain but also maintain boundaries: experience enough of the pain to be present, then leave intense personal processing for supervision so it does not intrude on the work.

instruction: How can I help my baby learn to sit up?
output: Once your baby can raise his chest, help him practice sitting up by holding him up or supporting his back with pillows as he learns to balance himself. He’ll soon learn to “tripod,” leaning forward and extending his arms to balance. Placing interesting toys in front of him will give him a focus as he gains balance.

instruction: I've been trying to suppress or push the thoughts away. Is that helpful?
output: Trying to dismiss, suppress, or block intrusive thoughts is usually counterproductive; suppression tends to increase preoccupation rather than reduce it.

instruction: Could what I'm feeling after birth be considered a trauma even if I don't know why I feel this way?
output: Many women with postpartum depression identify their experience as a trauma. The concept of trauma applies when the ego has been overwhelmed and unable to perform its normal functions; postpartum women can experience psychic injury of previously unknown proportion, especially if prior losses or history of depression are present.

instruction: Is it okay to let my baby play with empty plastic water bottles?
output: Empty plastic water bottles without a lid or small cap can be used as play things. Be sure there is no lid or small cap, as that could be a potential choking hazard.

instruction: Will pumping at work replace nursing for my baby?
output: At work you will pump just to supply milk and your baby will take a bottle just for food. It won’t be the same for either of you, but it will hold things together until you’re back together at the end of the day and nursing normally again.

instruction: Our older child seems jealous and acts out since the baby arrived — is that normal and what can we expect?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Siblings may feel neglected or jealous because of the attention shifting to the baby and because Mom may be tired after hospitalization and unable to play as much. Once the new baby arrives, you can expect your older child to be very proud and protective as well.

instruction: Can I lay my baby across my lap to burp him? How do I position him?
output: Lay the baby on your lap with their back up, support their head so it is higher than their chest, and gently pat or rotate your hand on their back.

instruction: Can my baby stay with me in the hospital if I need a procedure?
output: Hospital policies vary; in some cases your baby can stay with you if there is a second caregiver to be with the baby. Ask the hospital about their policy and possible arrangements to minimize missed feedings.

instruction: Did I say I only smoked pot a few times and didn't like how it made me feel?
output: You reported using marijuana a few times and that it made you feel paranoid and uncomfortable.

instruction: My breasts are very firm and painful four days after delivery — is this normal and what can I do?
output: Breasts often become firmer as milk comes in; by the second to fifth day you may have transitional milk and feel very full. Engorgement happens when breasts are overfilled with milk and fluids and can be uncomfortable or painful. The best approach is to nurse whenever your baby is hungry, feeding at both breasts about eight to twelve times per day or before breasts get swollen, firm, or painful. If breasts are so engorged the baby can’t latch, apply moist heat to soften them and, if needed, manually express some milk or use a pump before nursing. You can also try warm showers or a warm washcloth just before feeding to encourage milk flow, and cool compresses in between or after feeding if warmth doesn’t help or no milk is flowing. Gently massage from under the arm down to the nipple and express or pump just enough for comfort. Ibuprofen has been shown to be safe and effective for engorgement; take the dosage recommended by your doctor and don’t take other medications without approval.

instruction: We were using bottles in the hospital and came home partly bottle-feeding and partly breastfeeding. How did she switch to full breastfeeding?
output: After reviewing the situation, she found she was producing more milk than the baby needed for a day and that the baby was nursing well; she then stopped bottles and transitioned to full-time breastfeeding.

instruction: My newborn's head has a soft swollen area from delivery. Is it serious and how long will it take to go away?
output: This swelling represents bleeding along the outer side of the skull bones, not inside the brain, and is not serious. It usually takes six to ten weeks to disappear.

instruction: Why might my labor become more difficult in a hospital setting?
output: Hospital routines, bright lights, unfamiliar smells, and clinical equipment can increase anxiety for some people, which can trigger stress hormones and make labor feel more difficult or slow down progress.

instruction: Is it okay to give my infant cow’s milk before their first birthday?
output: Cow’s milk is not recommended until after a year of age.

instruction: Why does my newborn want to nurse so much at night?
output: Newborns may need a lot of nursing at night at the beginning because their internal clock is backward; over time they gradually learn the difference between day and night.

instruction: What should I do if my breasts get engorged again after nursing?
output: If engorgement returns, try expressing as much milk as you can from both breasts to relieve the fullness and get down to the bottom of the build-up.

instruction: Does feeding help prevent jaundice, and how?
output: Yes. Bilirubin is excreted in stool, so adequate milk intake (not water) that promotes stooling helps prevent excessive jaundice. Jaundice from inadequate stooling tends to improve as milk becomes plentiful over the first days.

instruction: Could medication help me sleep if I can’t stop waking up or ruminating at night?
output: In some cases, short-term medication for sleep can help break a self-perpetuating cycle of sleeplessness, particularly when early morning wakening is a prominent symptom; this possibility should be discussed with a clinician.

instruction: I've tried everything and feel like giving up. Is there really more help out there for breastfeeding problems?
output: Yes. Experienced breastfeeding helpers often have practical tools, tips, and education from years of work that aren't always available in books or websites. If one helper isn't helping, try another; helpers vary in training and fit.

instruction: Can a sling be dangerous for my newborn's breathing?
output: Be careful that the baby is not curled up with his chin against his chest, which can restrict his breathing. Babies usually hate being dropped into a ‘black hole’ that squeezes their head, keeps them from seeing out, and can interfere with breathing.

instruction: How should caregivers handle a toddler who uses tantrums to get attention?
output: Avoid giving excessive attention for bad behavior; even negative attention can reinforce it. Make sure caregivers follow consistent policies and use strategies like ignoring attention-seeking misbehavior and using time-out rather than giving the child extra attention.

instruction: Why do hospitals encourage immediate breastfeeding right after a routine delivery?
output: Hospitals often encourage breastfeeding within the first hour after a routine delivery while the baby is held skin-to-skin. Breastfeeding right away causes the mother’s uterus to contract, which reduces uterine bleeding, and it gives the newborn immediate protection against infections. The same hormone that stimulates the milk ejection (let-down) response also triggers uterine contractions.

instruction: Is it okay to let my baby touch and play with food when you introduce solids?
output: Yes. Babies learn about solids through all their senses—touching, turning, examining, and squeezing are a normal part of learning about new tastes and textures.

instruction: How does breastfeeding help my baby besides giving milk?
output: Breastfeeding provides warmth, security, comfort, and closeness as well as nutrition. Human milk contains a complete mix of nutrients, adapts to the baby’s needs over time, includes living cells that inhibit harmful bacteria and viruses, and contains immune factors such as interferon and interleukins that help protect the infant from infection.

instruction: After my supply is established, how often will I need to pump?
How long should I try to breastfeed to reduce my baby's early risks of illness?
output: Try to make a serious goal of nursing for a month. Breastfeeding for four to six weeks helps ease your baby through the most hazardous part of infancy. Newborns who are not breastfed are much more likely to get sick or be hospitalized and have many more digestive problems than breastfed babies. After four to six weeks you’ll probably have worked through early nursing concerns.

instruction: I nursed and bottle-fed my older child. Will that change our relationship later on?
output: Many parents who have both nursed and bottle-fed notice differences in their relationship with their children. Some report that the close bond begun during breastfeeding continues to influence the relationship long after weaning.

instruction: I let my baby suck on a pacifier but then she sleeps — could that be a problem?
output: Sucking on a pacifier releases CCK, so a baby can fall asleep thinking she’s been fed. But because the breasts didn’t have that feeding’s milk removed, the result can be slower weight gain and lower milk supply.

instruction: How should I thaw my baby's frozen milk?
output: Thaw frozen milk in the refrigerator, in a bowl of warm water, or under a warm faucet.

instruction: Is it safe to buy a used consumer-grade pump from eBay or Craigslist?
output: Used consumer-grade pumps present concerns: many are not built for long-term use, so suction and cycling may degrade, and because they are not closed systems milk or moisture may have entered mechanical parts where microbes could grow. Consider the source carefully and, if using a used pump, at minimum replace the pump kit parts (tubing, flanges, valves) with new ones.

instruction: I had an unexpected C-section and I feel like something is missing — is that a normal reaction?
output: Yes. Feeling disconnected after a traumatic or unexpected birth is common. Nursing, skin-to-skin contact, baths together, massage, and quiet time with the baby often help the sense of connection grow over time.

instruction: What happens at a La Leche League meeting?
output: La Leche League meetings and similar breastfeeding support groups typically include pregnant women, mothers with newborns, and mothers with older infants and children. Meetings focus on answering questions, sharing experiences, and offering mutual support at different stages of breastfeeding.

instruction: My baby doesn’t seem to follow moving objects with her eyes yet. When is that concerning?
output: If your baby doesn’t follow moving objects with her eyes by two to three months, that is a warning sign to discuss with your pediatrician.

instruction: I feel glued to the couch while nursing my twins and sometimes I doze off. Is that normal?
output: Feeling anchored while nursing multiples is common. Some parents find a comfortable lying-down nursing position that allows brief rest; ensure safe sleep practices and stay mindful of baby positioning while resting.

instruction: How can I help keep my and my baby’s immune systems strong?
output: Take care of your own health. Adequate rest (if possible) and a diet rich in vitamin D, antioxidants, zinc, and omega‑3 fats can help keep your immune system in good shape, which also helps keep your baby’s in good shape.

instruction: What blood tests should be done at the twelve-month well-child exam?
output: At the 12-month well-child exam your child should have a hemoglobin (to screen for anemia) and a blood lead level (to assess for lead exposure). Iron-deficiency anemia can be common and iron supplementation may be needed if indicated.

instruction: Can I simply watch my baby for signs instead of weaning if I'm worried about the medicine?
output: Yes. When in doubt you can monitor the baby for changes (for example, increased fussiness or diarrhea) and seek testing or clinical advice if concerns arise; consult your clinician for guidance tailored to the specific medication.

instruction: Does nursing help with tantrums and getting my child to sleep?
output: Yes. Nursing often soothes tears and tantrums, and the hormones and enzymes released by nursing help gently bring a child to sleep. It’s an effective comfort and calming method for many situations.

instruction: Where can I find official guidance about breastfeeding during specific maternal diseases or vaccinations?
output: The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (cdc.gov) and the World Health Organization (who.int) both have information on breastfeeding during specific maternal diseases and vaccinations.

instruction: My 2-week-old's rectal temperature is 100.5°F. What should I do?
output: It’s understandable to be worried. Newborns can get very sick very quickly, so if your infant is under three months and has a rectal temperature of 100.4 degrees Fahrenheit (38 degrees Celsius) or higher, call your pediatrician.

instruction: What changes happen to my breasts during the first three months of pregnancy?
output: Breast growth accelerates: buds form into clusters of milk-making cells (alveoli), milk ducts develop, veins may become more visible, areolae may darken or enlarge, and small bumps (Montgomery glands) begin to form on the areolae. Breasts may feel tender and larger.

instruction: Are there foods I should never give a young child because of choking risk?
output: Never offer chunks of peanut butter, large pieces of raw vegetables, whole nuts, whole grapes, popcorn, uncooked peas, celery, gum, hard candies, or other hard or round foods that could pose a choking risk. Always observe your child while she is eating.

instruction: Should I avoid exposing my baby to stressful experiences?
output: Yes. Avoid subjecting your baby to stressful or traumatic experiences when possible. Provide a loving, comforting relationship and consistent care from caregivers to help your baby feel secure.

instruction: Can my partner call a breastfeeding helper for us, or does the mother need to call?
output: Breastfeeding helpers usually prefer the mother to call so they know she wants their involvement. However, if it’s only a few days post-birth and the mother can be heard in the background, helpers will often proceed based on that information.

instruction: Will the doctor watch my baby walk during visits?
output: Yes. Later, after the baby starts to walk, the doctor will watch her take a few steps to make sure the legs and feet are properly aligned and move normally.

instruction: I'm struggling with breastfeeding and feel ashamed. Should I try to fix it on my own or get help?
output: You can try small adjustments, but find a real breastfeeding support person — for example a La Leche League (LLL) Leader, an International Board Certified Lactation Consultant (IBCLC), or another helper trained in technical aspects of breastfeeding. Do it soon; a trained person can listen to your problem and talk you through solutions, and you may need more than one helper to get to a long, positive experience.

instruction: Do I need to stop nursing at bedtime so my toddler will sleep on their own?
output: Nursing to sleep is very normal at this age and not a bad habit. As children move from baby to toddler, many consolidate naps and then sleep for a reliable chunk of time provided they can nurse to sleep. A small baby often wants only nursing to sleep.

instruction: Are male therapists effective for treating postpartum depression?
output: Male therapists can be effective and are described as valuable adjunctively, for example in supporting partners. Therapists who are men, unmarried, or without children can successfully treat women with postpartum depression, though many women report a preference for female therapists.

instruction: Can I feed my baby on demand even though I'm using bottles of pumped milk?
output: Yes. Being bottlefed does not prevent feeding on demand; you can respond to your baby’s hunger cues and offer milk as needed.

instruction: Why does my baby seem to prefer black-and-white pictures and bold patterns?
output: Babies prefer black-and-white or high-contrast patterns such as stripes, bull’s-eyes, checks, and very simple faces because high contrast attracts their attention.

instruction: How often should I read to my baby?
output: Read to your baby every day.

instruction: I'm going back to work soon. Any simple ways to feel more rested?
output: Try taking the baby to bed with you an hour earlier than you would normally turn in. That extra hour being horizontal in a dark room, even if you don't sleep through all of it, can leave you much better rested the next day.

instruction: How should I handle a baby who is already crying when I try to feed her?
output: Once she’s crying she’ll have a harder time latching. Calm her down before trying to feed her, and try to answer her earlier requests instead of waiting until she demands food.

instruction: What did LLL recommend about introducing solid foods?
output: The founders recommended postponing the introduction of solids to about six months, based on medical research and their own experience watching for solids readiness. Today the World Health Organization and many health organizations agree.

instruction: Who can help teach oral stimulation exercises to improve suction in a baby with Down syndrome?
output: A board-certified lactation consultant can teach oral stimulation exercises to help improve a baby’s suction; local support groups can also offer practical support.

instruction: How should I support my baby when I'm sitting up to nurse?
output: Hold him behind his back and shoulders with his front to your front. Tuck his shoulders in closely to you so he feels stable. If he feels unstable he may wave his arms, which can make latching much more difficult.

instruction: Can using the wrong pumping setting cause nipple soreness?
output: Yes. For example, using a double‑pumping setting while single‑pumping can cause soreness. Make sure the pump is set up correctly for how you’re using it.

instruction: If I missed Tdap while pregnant, can I get it right after delivery?
output: Yes. If the vaccine is not given during pregnancy, it should be given to the mom immediately after delivery.

instruction: My baby keeps nibbling or pulling away while nursing. Could pacifiers or bottles be the cause?
output: Babies may nibble or chew with their jaws instead of using the tongue, or show frustration by pulling away or crying. Researchers are still uncertain whether artificial nipples cause breastfeeding problems or are a response to a problem that already existed. Some experts recommend avoiding bottles and pacifiers for the first several weeks until breastfeeding is going well.

instruction: How can I keep my milk supply up while the baby is learning to nurse?
output: Removing milk from your breasts is as important as feeding the baby. Express or pump at least 8–10 times per 24 hours (including at least once at night), massage before and/or during pumping, hand-express afterward, and consider expressing extra and storing it in the fridge to be a feeding or two ahead. Stay in touch with your Leader or other breastfeeding support. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: What does it mean when someone calls a baby a "sparkler," and how should I care for one?
output: A "sparkler" is an intense, sensitive baby who needs lots of input and tends to be either on high or off. Keep your sense of humor, avoid comparing him to placid babies, look for other mothers of sparklers to share notes, and keep your little sparkler close—your presence and touch will often help calm him.

instruction: Are there breastfeeding specialists available in hospitals to help me?
output: Many hospitals with maternity services have a breastfeeding specialist (lactation consultant) on staff who can assist you with latch, positioning, pumping, and supply concerns.

instruction: What are the benefits of laid-back breastfeeding?
output: Laid-back breastfeeding gives your baby full, consistent support with no gaps or pressure points. Gravity brings your baby toward your breast as he bobs, instead of pulling his head away. Your baby’s whole body touching yours triggers instinctive responses. He’s less likely to flail his arms because he feels secure, and you can be more relaxed with at least one hand free.

instruction: If my family has few resources and my depression is severe, what options are mentioned?
output: When families are especially vulnerable or lack resources, home-based interventions or more child-focused treatment approaches may be appropriate to support both mother and baby.

instruction: My baby seems reluctant to latch after bottle feeding. What can I try to entice her back to the breast?
output: Be patient and gentle. Increase skin-to-skin time, offer the breast when she is drowsy or waking, try laid-back positions, smear a little milk on the areola, or use an at-breast supplementer or nipple shield if appropriate. Consult a lactation specialist for personalized strategies and avoid using pacifiers while retraining feeding.

instruction: What is galactosemia and how is it checked in newborns?
output: Galactosemia is a rare disorder in which children have an intolerance to galactose, one of the two sugars in lactose; children with this condition cannot tolerate breast milk and must be fed a lactose-free formula. All states include a blood test for galactosemia in routine newborn screening after birth.

instruction: Should I use a nursing cover or shawl in public?
output: A blanket or specially designed cover can sometimes attract attention; many mothers find nursing without a cover draws less notice and is more practical.

instruction: Can I get pregnant before my first postpartum period returns?
output: It is possible to ovulate before your first postpartum period, so pregnancy can occur before you have a period. If you want to avoid pregnancy, consider using contraception even before periods resume.

instruction: Should I use baby talk with my infant or speak like an adult?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. It’s easy to fall into baby talk sometimes, but you should try mixing your conversations with adult language and eventually phase out the baby talk.

instruction: If I taste my milk to check it, is that something others do?
output: Yes. Some parents taste a small amount of their milk to check flavor or changes; this is a personal choice.

instruction: My 18-month-old hits and bites when frustrated. What should I do to stop this aggressive behavior?
output: Before age two, many children show frustration by kicking, biting, or hitting. Supervise closely, set firm and consistent limits, provide plenty of active play, praise good behavior, model appropriate interactions, and remind him of expectations in advance. Time-out or time-in can be used when needed.

instruction: My baby was small at birth — will they need tests or extra monitoring?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. A small baby may need temperature, blood glucose, and hemoglobin level to be closely monitored. After birth the pediatrician will thoroughly evaluate a small infant and decide when she is ready to go home.

instruction: My baby refused to nurse after I came back from the hospital—is that normal?
output: Sometimes after a separation of more than a day a baby will refuse to nurse and seem to reject his mother; most of these breast refusals are temporary. Gentle reacquainting time, calming activities (such as a warm bath together or quiet skin-to-skin contact), and shared rest can help soothe the baby. It is normal to feel upset by this.

instruction: Do official organizations recommend how long to breastfeed?
output: Yes. UNICEF, the World Health Organization, and the Canadian Paediatric Society recommend breastfeeding for at least the first two years of life and beyond for as long as mutually desired.

instruction: Should I wear breast shells all day to protect sore nipples?
output: You can use breast shells to protect a sore nipple, but they tend to hold external moisture which can slow healing. Don’t wear them at night because they can slide and cause further damage. Wash and dry them frequently to prevent bacterial growth. Leave them off as much as possible to speed healing, and you can use one while nursing to keep the baby from kicking a sore nipple.

instruction: Will I have to wash and sterilize bottles if I choose to breastfeed?
output: If you exclusively breastfeed directly at the breast, you avoid the routine washing and sterilizing of bottles required for formula feeding. If you express milk and feed by bottle, bottle cleaning will still be needed.

instruction: Does breast size affect my ability to make milk?
output: Breast size says nothing at all about your ability to make milk. However, there may need to be some matching of parts between your breasts and your baby for optimal latch.

instruction: Is it harmful if the hospital keeps my baby in a central nursery away from me at night?
output: Having your baby away from you can reduce opportunities for feeding cues, may slow milk coming in, and can make it harder for you to control supplementation and monitor the baby. Many families find rooming-in supports feeding and bonding.

instruction: Can hand expression be a quick method for a busy schedule?
output: Yes. With practice some mothers can hand-express quickly; initial sessions may take longer, but many become efficient with technique and routine.

instruction: I'm having trouble getting my newborn settled in the sling. Any tips?
output: Start with a snug sling so your newborn is stabilized in a fairly upright position with his head about level with your breasts, not down by your waist. Arrange the baby with his face above the level of the sling, start moving as soon as he’s settled, and pat him through the fabric and talk to him. Non-adjustable slings can be shifted to find a good angle and may need a thin blanket in the bottom for a newborn.

instruction: Is teething likely to make breastfeeding painful?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. For the most part, teeth are irrelevant to breastfeeding. However, when teeth are first coming through some babies try to bite and there can occasionally be pain during nursing; you may also need to wiggle your baby into a more comfortable nursing position at those times.

instruction: Is it okay to nurse twins at the same time or should I do them separately?
output: Either approach is fine. Tandem nursing can save time, but nursing separately can simplify latching. Try both and see what best fits your comfort and the babies’ needs.

instruction: Any small tricks to help milk let-down when I'm distracted or have other children around?
output: Some parents find smelling something their baby has worn or keeping photos or videos of the baby to look at while pumping helps. Other calming cues that remind you of the baby, such as songs or routines, may also help milk release.

instruction: Should I avoid peanuts while breastfeeding or delay introducing peanuts to my infant?
output: Introducing peanut products to infants between four and six months of age may prevent a developing peanut allergy. Mothers who breastfeed do not need to avoid certain foods, because breast milk helps the baby become tolerant of common allergens. If your child has severe eczema or an allergy to egg proteins, talk to your doctor first. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: When do babies usually start transferring toys from one hand to the other?
output: By four months babies may be able to transfer objects from hand to hand.

instruction: My nipple is inverted — what can I try to draw it out?
output: Try working your nipple out by hand and then nursing. Some inverted nipples self-correct during pregnancy, and there are commercial devices that help evert the nipple before or after birth. Pumping briefly before nursing may also help. One suction device option is a needleless syringe: remove the plunger, cut the needle end off, put the plunger in the wrong end, pull out on the plunger far enough to draw the nipple out without discomfort, keep the suction up for about a minute, then depress the plunger to stop the suction and offer the breast. If the nipple won’t evert at all, breastfeeding may be more difficult, but it takes only one nipple to breastfeed.

instruction: Do working mothers usually pump when nursing a toddler?
output: Most working mothers of toddlers are not pumping regularly at this stage. Toddlers usually have other sources of nutrition and the mother may have more flexibility in arranging her day.

instruction: I haven't found a comfortable nursing position yet. Will I ever find one that fits me?
output: You may find a style tailored to your breast shape, body, and your baby’s preferences. With time you and your baby will develop positions that feel comfortable.

instruction: What are common reasons a baby is born small?
output: Common causes of a baby being born small include being born early (preterm); being born to small parents; the mother’s ethnicity; fetal chromosomal abnormalities; inadequate weight gain in pregnancy; the mother’s chronic diseases such as high blood pressure or heart or kidney disease; placental issues that lead the baby to be undernourished; the mother’s alcohol or substance use during pregnancy; and the mother’s smoking during pregnancy.

instruction: What does 'cradle and rock gently' mean when holding my newborn?
output: Cradle and rock him gently. Do not jerk or shake the baby.

instruction: My 3-month-old stares at her hands for long periods — is that normal?
output: Yes. During the early months babies often stare at their hands for stretches of time and find their fingers amusing, and many babies do this at three months.

instruction: Since his first birthday my toddler eats much less. Is that normal and how much does he need?
output: A drop in appetite after the first birthday is common because growth rate slows. Toddlers generally need about 1,000 calories a day, which can be divided among three meals and two snacks. Eating patterns are often erratic and unpredictable.

instruction: Should I put moisturizer on my baby after a bath?
output: Pat your baby dry and apply a fragrance-free, hypoallergenic moisturizer immediately after bathing; this can help prevent eczema.

instruction: I had breast implants. Will that stop me from breastfeeding?
output: Breast enlargement surgery usually does not interfere with breastfeeding provided the breasts were previously normal, the nipples were not moved, and no ducts were cut.

instruction: Should I be pumping at night?
output: Pumping at least once at night during the first six weeks helps establish supply. After that, whether you pump at night depends on how long you can go without breasts becoming uncomfortably full and how much you can express during daytime sessions to meet your baby’s needs.

instruction: How can we help our older child adjust to a new baby at home?
output: Many parents worry about this. Try to reassure siblings that they’re still loved and valued. If possible, have the siblings visit mother and baby in the hospital. When Mom comes home, bring each sibling a special gift. Set aside a special time to spend alone with each sibling every day, and make sure both parents have time with each child individually and together.

instruction: Why does milk sometimes seem to spurt quickly when my baby nurses?
output: Oxytocin causes the muscles around the alveoli to squeeze, building pressure and opening ducts so milk can flow quickly or in spurts during let-down.

instruction: Could sore nipples be an early sign of pregnancy?
output: Yes. Sore nipples can be an early sign of pregnancy in some people.

instruction: I'm worried nursing my toddler seems endless. Is 'he's too old' a good reason to stop?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Saying 'he's too old' isn't a very logical reason to stop; there usually isn't anything solid that comes after that 'because.'

instruction: Should I feed a two-month-old as soon as he cues for feeding?
output: Yes. It’s important to feed a two-month-old when he shows feeding cues so he receives enough milk. Nursing a newborn is largely a physical routine of feeding and care.

instruction: Why does my baby smile when she looks at my eyes?
output: The human face is a baby's favorite image and babies are drawn automatically to faces, particularly the eyes. Often the mere sight of your eyes will make a baby smile.

instruction: What are some simple ways to stimulate language and thinking during daily routines?
output: Talk or sing during dressing, bathing, feeding, play, walking, and driving. Speak slowly, allow time for responses, listen and expand your child’s phrases, ask questions, and read daily. Play calm, melodic music to support attention and mood.

instruction: If I do nothing after birth and don't want to breastfeed, will my body still make milk?
output: Yes. The physiological processes that produce milk proceed whether or not milk is removed. Milk production will continue only as long as milk is regularly removed from the breasts.

instruction: The parents and I disagree about discipline. What should I do when I'm caring for my grandchild?
output: Discuss discipline with the baby’s parents and make sure your approach is consistent with their wishes. Your continued role is to assist your child to become an effective parent, so follow the parents’ guidance on discipline.

instruction: If my baby stops the morning nap, should I change his bedtime?
output: You can move nighttime bedtime slightly earlier (about 20 to 30 minutes) and start the afternoon nap a little sooner as the morning nap fades.

instruction: How can I safely break my baby's suction if I need to get up for a moment?
output: Slide a finger into the corner of your baby’s mouth to break the suction. Use a finger about the size of your nipple, slide it into the corner between the gums, and twist gently before removing the nipple.

instruction: Who reviews La Leche League's medical information?
output: LLL has a Professional Advisory Board with a Health Advisory Council composed of doctors and health professionals from around the world. LLL consults that council about medical situations, evaluation of new research, and for review of most LLL publications that include medical information. Those efforts are complemented by doctors around the world who serve as LLL Medical Associates. A Legal Advisory Council and a Management Advisory Council complete the Professional Advisory Board.

instruction: I’ve been feeling anxious and unhappy since my baby was born. How long should I wait before talking to my doctor?
output: If feelings of anxiety, unhappiness, or depression continue for more than a few days or interfere with your ability to care for yourself or your baby, talk to your doctor or a mental-health professional.

instruction: I heard some bras are more comfortable than others for nursing — what fabric should I look for?
output: Cotton is often the most comfortable fabric for nursing bras, and higher-quality bras generally hold up better to frequent washing and wear.

instruction: Do all newborns need their blood sugar checked after birth?
output: Screening practices vary. Babies with no risk factors or symptoms typically do not need routine screening, while at-risk babies should be monitored until blood sugar normalizes.

instruction: I heard misoprostol is used to ripen the cervix. Is that safe?
output: Misoprostol (Cytotec) can cause very intense contractions and, in some situations (for example with a prior Cesarean scar), can be associated with serious complications. Its use as a cervical ripener is considered controversial in some settings; discuss risks and alternatives with your provider.

instruction: I’m worried I won’t make enough milk — how likely is that?
output: Most mothers are able to make plenty of milk. Some do have difficulties, but many causes of low supply are fixable with support and changes in feeding or milk removal.

instruction: How do I get the right size and fit for a nipple shield?
output: Experiment to see what size works best; premature babies may do best with the smallest comfortable size. You can try turning the shield almost inside out so the stiff nipple section is folded back, place it so your nipple is closer to the shield’s tip, spread the rim into place (moistening underneath may help it stick), and wiggle the nipple section to pop it out for a better fit.

instruction: How effective is the Lactational Amenorrhea Method (LAM) at preventing pregnancy when used correctly?
output: LAM is 98 to 99 percent effective in preventing pregnancy when all three LAM conditions are met (baby under six months, periods haven’t resumed, and exclusive breastfeeding).

instruction: Can I catch a cold from my baby, or can my baby catch mine before I have symptoms?
output: The infection spreads easily through respiratory droplets or by hand contact, and people can spread most viruses before they develop symptoms, so you won’t be able to avoid all colds.

instruction: Can I get a new tattoo while I'm still breastfeeding? I'm nervous about it.
output: Tattooing while breastfeeding carries an infection risk and many tattooists prefer to wait until after you’ve weaned. It is often suggested to wait at least until your child’s first birthday to give your body time to recover from childbirth and reduce potential complications.

instruction: I sometimes have scary intrusive thoughts. Does that mean I'm psychotic or will lose my baby?
output: Many parents worry about this. Having scary or intrusive thoughts can be frightening, but postpartum depression and postpartum psychosis are different illnesses. Women with postpartum depression may feel as though they are going crazy, but they do not become psychotic; they do not 'snap' and lose touch with reality.

instruction: Some disposable diapers have a wetness stripe—what should I know about it?
output: Some disposable diapers include a strip that changes color to indicate wetness. Disposable diapers generally have an inner liner to help keep wetness away from the skin, an absorbent core, and an outer waterproof covering.

instruction: Can I try switching to the breast in the middle of a feeding if my baby is latched to the shield?
output: Yes; try removing the shield in the middle of a nursing session during a calm time. If he starts to get upset, give him the shield and try again later.

instruction: Is it normal that an older baby doesn’t fall asleep every time at the breast?
output: Yes. An older baby is less likely to fall asleep at every nursing; sometimes he nurses and then lets go and is ready to interact or play, and other times he nurses until he falls asleep.

instruction: When do babies usually give their first true smile?
output: By the beginning of the second month many babies give their first true smile.

instruction: We use well water at home — should I worry about fluoride for my baby?
output: Have your well tested to determine the amount of natural fluoride. After six months, infants need appropriate fluoride supplementation only if local drinking water contains less than 0.3 ppm fluoride, and your pediatrician or pediatric dentist can advise about fluoride drops.

instruction: Is hand expression helpful for a plugged duct or bleb?
